prov_royal.title: The Purpose of Proverbs and Wisdom's First Call
prov_royal.synopsis: Proverbs opens with its purpose statement: 'To know wisdom (hokhmah) and instruction (musar), to understand words of insight' (1:2). The foundational axiom follows: 'The fear of YHWH is the beginning of knowledge (da'at); fools despise wisdom and instruction' (1:7). This verse is the theological key to the entire book -- and arguably to all wisdom literature. True knowledge does not begin with observation or investigation but with a posture of reverence before the Creator. The chapter then presents the first of many warnings: do not follow sinners who promise easy wealth through violence (1:10-19). The chapter climaxes with Wisdom personified as a woman crying out in the public square: 'How long, O simple ones, will you love being simple?' (1:22). Those who reject her call will be destroyed; those who listen 'will dwell secure and will be at ease, without dread of disaster' (1:33).

key_verse.text: The fear of the LORD is the beginning of knowledge; fools despise wisdom and instruction.

hebrew_terms: hokhmah (wisdom -- skill for living, moral and practical competence, the central concept of the book)
musar (discipline/instruction -- the correction and training that produces wisdom)
yirat YHWH (fear of YHWH -- reverent awe before God, the foundation of all knowledge)
da'at (knowledge -- not merely information but relational knowledge rooted in the fear of YHWH)
mashal (proverb/comparison -- the literary genre of the book, wisdom through analogy)
prov_royal.ane_backdrop: The 'instruction' (musar) genre has deep roots in ANE literature. Egyptian wisdom texts (Instructions of Ptahhotep, ~2400 BC; Instructions of Amenemope, ~1200 BC) address 'my son' with practical and moral guidance, just as Proverbs does. The Mesopotamian 'Instructions of Shuruppak' (~2600 BC) is the oldest known wisdom text, offering similar parental counsel. The personification of Wisdom calling in the streets (1:20-21) has possible parallels in Egyptian Ma'at (cosmic order/truth personified as a goddess) and Mesopotamian Nisaba (goddess of wisdom and writing), though Proverbs' Wisdom is not a goddess but an attribute of YHWH personified.

second_temple.summary: Ben Sira opens with 'All wisdom comes from the Lord and is with him forever' and develops the 'fear of the Lord' theme extensively, identifying wisdom with Torah obedience.
second_temple.relevance: Shows how the later wisdom tradition developed Proverbs 1:7's axiom, making the connection between wisdom and Torah explicit.

second_temple.summary: Wisdom is described as 'a breath of the power of God, a pure emanation of the glory of the Almighty' who 'orders all things well.'
second_temple.relevance: Develops Proverbs' personified Wisdom into a more explicit hypostasis, bridging toward the Logos christology of John 1.

cross_refs.note: 'The fear of YHWH is the beginning of wisdom' -- the parallel formulation that bookends the prologue
'The fear of the Lord, that is wisdom' -- Job's wisdom hymn reaches the same conclusion
'The fear of YHWH is the beginning of wisdom' -- the psalmic expression of the same axiom
'If any of you lacks wisdom, let him ask God' -- wisdom as divine gift, accessible through relationship
In Christ 'are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge' -- the christological fulfillment

prov_royal.divine_council_note: The 'fear of YHWH' (yirat YHWH, 1:7) is not mere emotion but a posture of recognition: YHWH is the sovereign of the cosmos and the divine council, and human beings approach true knowledge only by acknowledging this reality. Wisdom's public call (1:20-33) positions her as a divine herald -- a messenger from the council's throne room, bringing the Creator's invitation to the human world. Those who reject her reject the cosmic order itself.

sections.heading: The Purpose Statement and the Fear of YHWH (1:1-7)
sections.body: The prologue declares the book's comprehensive purpose: wisdom (hokhmah), instruction (musar), understanding (binah), wise dealing (haskel), righteousness (tsedeq), justice (mishpat), and equity (mesharim). These are not abstract qualities but practical competencies for living well in God's ordered world. The foundational axiom -- 'the fear of YHWH is the beginning of knowledge' (1:7) -- establishes that wisdom is theological before it is practical. The word 'reshit' (beginning) means both 'starting point' and 'chief part': the fear of YHWH is both where wisdom begins and what wisdom essentially is.

sections.heading: Warning Against Violent Sinners (1:8-19)
sections.body: The first instruction addresses the temptation of easy wealth through violence: 'If sinners entice you, do not consent. If they say, "Come with us, let us lie in wait for blood"' (1:10-11). The sinners promise a common purse (1:14) -- shared wealth from shared crime. The father's counsel: 'Do not walk in the way with them; hold back your foot from their paths, for their feet run to evil' (1:15-16). The metaphor of the bird stepping into a net (1:17) illustrates the self-destructive nature of violent gain: 'They lie in wait for their own blood; they set an ambush for their own lives' (1:18). This establishes the retribution principle that pervades the book.

sections.heading: Wisdom's Public Call (1:20-33)
sections.body: Wisdom personified makes her first appearance: 'Wisdom cries aloud in the street; in the markets she raises her voice; at the head of the noisy streets she cries out; at the entrance of the city gates she speaks' (1:20-21). The locations are public -- the street, the marketplace, the city gate (where legal and civic business was conducted). Wisdom is not esoteric or hidden; she calls to everyone. Her message is urgent: 'How long, O simple ones, will you love being simple? How long will scoffers delight in their scoffing and fools hate knowledge?' (1:22). She promises the spirit of understanding to those who respond (1:23) but warns of devastating consequences for those who refuse: 'I also will laugh at your calamity; I will mock when terror strikes you' (1:26). The passage presents a cosmic choice: respond to Wisdom and live, or refuse and perish.

prov_royal.title: The Path of Wisdom and the Path of Darkness
prov_royal.synopsis: Chapters 2-4 develop the Two Ways theology: the path of wisdom leading to life and the path of evil leading to death. Chapter 2 is a single extended sentence in Hebrew: IF you seek wisdom as silver and search for it as hidden treasure (2:1-4), THEN you will understand the fear of YHWH and find the knowledge of God (2:5-8), and wisdom will guard you from the evil man (2:12-15) and the forbidden woman (2:16-19). Chapter 3 presents the blessings of wisdom in creation-laden terms: 'YHWH by wisdom founded the earth; by understanding he established the heavens' (3:19). Chapter 4 recalls the father's own instruction by his father: 'When I was a son with my father, tender, the only one in the sight of my mother, he taught me and said to me, "Let your heart hold fast my words"' (4:3-4). The climax is the famous contrast: 'The path of the righteous is like the light of dawn, which shines brighter and brighter until full day. The way of the wicked is like deep darkness; they do not know over what they stumble' (4:18-19).

key_verse.text: The LORD by wisdom founded the earth; by understanding he established the heavens; by his knowledge the deeps broke open, and the clouds drop down the dew.

hebrew_terms: derek (way/path -- the moral direction of life, the central metaphor of wisdom literature)
chesed (steadfast love/loyalty -- coupled with truth as the core character qualities, 3:3)
matmon (hidden treasure -- wisdom is worth the most diligent search, 2:4)
lev (heart -- the seat of the will and intellect, to be guarded above all else, 4:23)
prov_royal.ane_backdrop: The 'Two Ways' motif is attested in Egyptian wisdom literature (the 'hot' man vs. the 'silent' man in Amenemope) and in Mesopotamian texts that contrast the wise and foolish paths. The description of wisdom's role in creation (3:19-20) anticipates the fuller development in chapter 8 and connects to ANE concepts of cosmic order (Egyptian Ma'at, Mesopotamian Me) that were established at creation and govern the proper functioning of society.

second_temple.summary: The Qumran Community Rule describes the 'Two Spirits' and 'Two Ways' -- the spirit of truth vs. the spirit of falsehood, with each leading to distinct moral outcomes.
second_temple.relevance: The Two Ways theology of Proverbs 2-4 influenced the dualistic framework of the Qumran community, showing the enduring impact of wisdom theology on Second Temple Judaism.

cross_refs.note: Jesus' Two Ways: 'the gate is narrow and the way is hard that leads to life' -- the wisdom tradition's path imagery
'I have set before you today life and good, death and evil' -- the covenantal Two Ways theology Proverbs develops
The Two Ways psalm: the blessed man vs. the wicked, the tree by water vs. the chaff
'In him was life, and the life was the light of men. The light shines in the darkness' -- the Wisdom-Logos light/darkness motif

prov_royal.divine_council_note: Proverbs 3:19-20 establishes that wisdom is not merely a human pursuit but a cosmic reality: 'YHWH by wisdom founded the earth; by understanding he established the heavens.' Wisdom is the operating principle of creation itself. In the divine council framework, this means that the order wisdom teaches is not cultural convention but the reflection of the cosmic order YHWH embedded in creation. To follow wisdom is to align with the way reality was designed to work; to reject wisdom is to rebel against the created order.

sections.heading: The Great If-Then of Wisdom (2:1-22)
sections.body: Chapter 2 is architecturally elegant: a single conditional sentence spanning 22 verses. The protasis (if-clause, 2:1-4) demands active pursuit: 'If you receive my words... call out for insight... seek it like silver... search for it as for hidden treasures.' Wisdom is not passive -- it requires effort equal to mining for precious metals (cf. Job 28). The apodosis (then-clause) delivers triple protection: understanding the fear of YHWH (2:5-8), being delivered from the evil man (2:12-15), and being saved from the forbidden woman (2:16-19). The 'forbidden woman' (ishah zarah) is both a literal adulteress and a symbol of the alternative to Lady Wisdom -- the anti-wisdom path that leads to death.

sections.heading: Trust YHWH and Wisdom's Role in Creation (3:1-35)
sections.body: Chapter 3 opens with the famous exhortation: 'Trust in YHWH with all your heart, and do not lean on your own understanding. In all your ways acknowledge him, and he will make straight your paths' (3:5-6). This is the practical application of the 'fear of YHWH' axiom: wisdom is relational before it is intellectual. The chapter then makes a cosmic claim: 'YHWH by wisdom founded the earth; by understanding he established the heavens' (3:19). Wisdom is not a human invention but a divine attribute woven into creation's fabric. The discipline of God is presented positively: 'Do not despise YHWH's discipline (musar) or be weary of his reproof, for YHWH reproves him whom he loves, as a father the son in whom he delights' (3:11-12) -- quoted in Hebrews 12:5-6.

sections.heading: The Two Paths: Light and Darkness (4:1-27)
sections.body: The father transmits multigenerational wisdom: 'When I was a son with my father... he taught me' (4:3-4). The chain of wisdom instruction connects the generations. The centerpiece is 4:23: 'Keep your heart (lev) with all vigilance, for from it flow the springs of life.' The heart is the command center of the person -- will, intellect, and emotion. The chapter climaxes with the iconic contrast: 'The path of the righteous is like the light of dawn, which shines brighter and brighter until full day. The way of the wicked is like deep darkness; they do not know over what they stumble' (4:18-19). The imagery foreshadows John's light/darkness theology and Jesus' self-identification as the 'light of the world' (John 8:12).

prov_royal.title: The Forbidden Woman and the Call to Faithfulness
prov_royal.synopsis: Chapters 5-7 form an extended warning against sexual infidelity. The 'forbidden woman' (ishah zarah) represents both a literal temptation and the symbolic alternative to Lady Wisdom. Chapter 5 describes her allure: 'Her lips drip honey, and her speech is smoother than oil, but in the end she is bitter as wormwood' (5:3-4). The antidote is marital faithfulness: 'Drink water from your own cistern... Let your fountain be blessed, and rejoice in the wife of your youth' (5:15, 18). Chapter 6 includes a numerical saying about seven things YHWH hates (6:16-19) and warns that adultery's consequences are irreversible. Chapter 7 presents the most vivid narrative in Proverbs: the naive young man lured by the seductress in the twilight, going 'as an ox goes to the slaughter' (7:22). The theological point is that rejecting wisdom's discipline in any area leads to the same destination: death.

key_verse.text: For the lips of a forbidden woman drip honey, and her speech is smoother than oil, but in the end she is bitter as wormwood, sharp as a two-edged sword.

hebrew_terms: ishah zarah (forbidden/strange woman -- foreign to wisdom, representing the anti-wisdom path)
nofet (honey -- the initial sweetness of temptation that conceals destruction)
bor (cistern/well -- the metaphor for marital faithfulness as a contained, pure water source)
retsach (murder -- the husband's wrath that adultery provokes, showing the social devastation)
prov_royal.ane_backdrop: Warnings against the seductress are common in ANE wisdom literature. The Egyptian 'Instruction of Ani' warns extensively against the 'woman from abroad.' The Mesopotamian 'Counsels of Wisdom' similarly warns against the prostitute and the married woman. In the Proverbs context, the 'forbidden woman' functions on multiple levels: she is a literal temptation, a symbol of folly personified (Woman Folly of chapter 9), and possibly an allusion to the foreign goddess cults that drew Israel away from YHWH -- the same adultery/idolatry connection found in Hosea and Ezekiel.

second_temple.source: 4Q184 (Wiles of the Wicked Woman from Qumran)
second_temple.summary: A fragmentary text describing a wicked woman who sits at the city gates to entrap the righteous. She is associated with darkness and death.
second_temple.relevance: Develops Proverbs 5-7's forbidden-woman tradition into an explicitly demonic figure, showing how Qumran read these warnings in a spiritual-warfare framework.

cross_refs.note: Jesus on adultery of the heart -- intensifying the Proverbs warning to the level of internal desire
Israel's infidelity to YHWH portrayed as adultery -- the prophetic application of the wisdom warning
'Let marriage be held in honor among all' -- the NT's positive sexual ethic that Proverbs 5 establishes
Jezebel seducing God's servants -- the eschatological Woman Folly

prov_royal.divine_council_note: The forbidden woman in Proverbs functions as the human-level expression of cosmic rebellion against YHWH's order. In the divine council framework, the nations were led astray by the rebellious 'sons of God' (Deut 32:8-9); here, the individual is led astray by the seductress who represents the anti-wisdom principle. The contrast between Woman Wisdom (who leads to life) and Woman Folly (who leads to death) mirrors the cosmic choice between YHWH's order and the chaos of rebellion.

sections.heading: The Forbidden Woman's Honey and Wormwood (5:1-23)
sections.body: The contrast is stark: the forbidden woman's lips drip honey (5:3) but her end is bitter as wormwood (5:4). Her feet 'go down to death; her steps follow the path to Sheol' (5:5). The antidote is not asceticism but faithful marriage: 'Drink water from your own cistern, flowing water from your own well' (5:15). The cistern/well imagery is intimate: sexual faithfulness is compared to a contained, private water source that should not be scattered publicly (5:16). The chapter concludes with the theological foundation: 'For a man's ways are before the eyes of YHWH, and he ponders all his paths' (5:21). God sees what is done in darkness.

sections.heading: Warnings and Seven Abominations (6:1-35)
sections.body: Chapter 6 opens with practical warnings about surety (guaranteeing another's debt, 6:1-5), laziness (the ant parable, 6:6-11), and the worthless person (6:12-15). Then comes the numerical saying: 'There are six things that YHWH hates, seven that are an abomination to him: haughty eyes, a lying tongue, hands that shed innocent blood, a heart that devises wicked plans, feet that make haste to run to evil, a false witness who breathes out lies, and one who sows discord among brothers' (6:16-19). The social destructiveness of these seven sins explains why YHWH hates them: they dissolve the community bonds that wisdom builds.

sections.heading: The Naive Youth and the Seductress (7:1-27)
sections.body: Chapter 7 is the most vivid narrative in Proverbs. The sage watches from his window as a naive young man walks past the seductress's corner at twilight (7:6-9). She meets him 'dressed as a prostitute, wily of heart' (7:10). Her speech is a masterpiece of deception: she has offered her peace offerings (7:14 -- she has fulfilled her religious duties!), her bed is prepared with Egyptian linen and myrrh (7:16-17), and her husband is away on a long journey (7:19-20). 'With much seductive speech she persuades him; with her smooth talk she compels him. All at once he follows her, as an ox goes to the slaughter' (7:21-22). The conclusion: 'Her house is the way to Sheol, going down to the chambers of death' (7:27). The narrative is a parable of every wrong choice: attractive, compelling, and lethal.

prov_royal.title: Wisdom's Self-Revelation and the Two Banquets
prov_royal.synopsis: Chapter 8 is the theological summit of Proverbs and one of the most important passages in the entire Old Testament. Wisdom speaks in her own voice, declaring her presence at creation: 'YHWH possessed me at the beginning of his work... when he established the heavens, I was there' (8:22, 27). She was beside God as 'amon' (master craftsman or daily delight), 'rejoicing before him always, rejoicing in his inhabited world and delighting in the children of man' (8:30-31). Her invitation is life itself: 'Whoever finds me finds life and obtains favor from YHWH' (8:35). Chapter 9 presents the climactic choice: Woman Wisdom has built her house with seven pillars and prepared a banquet (9:1-6); Woman Folly sits at her door offering stolen water (9:13-18). Between them stands the axiom: 'The fear of YHWH is the beginning of wisdom, and the knowledge of the Holy One is insight' (9:10). The prologue is complete.

key_verse.text: The LORD possessed me at the beginning of his work, the first of his acts of old. Ages ago I was set up, at the first, before the beginning of the earth... then I was beside him, like a master workman, and I was daily his delight, rejoicing before him always, rejoicing in his inhabited world and delighting in the children of man.

hebrew_terms: qanani (possessed/created/acquired me -- the debated verb of 8:22, central to christological controversy)
amon (master workman/nursling/constantly -- the debated role of Wisdom at creation, 8:30)
nissakhti (I was set up/appointed/poured out -- possibly an enthronement term, 8:23)
qedoshim (Holy Ones -- 'knowledge of the Holy One/Ones' in 9:10, possibly a divine council reference)
sheva ammudim (seven pillars -- Wisdom's house has seven pillars, 9:1, symbolizing cosmic completeness)
prov_royal.ane_backdrop: The personification of Wisdom calling in the public square and claiming a role in creation has parallels in Egyptian Ma'at (cosmic order personified as a goddess who was the daughter of the sun-god Re and present at creation) and in Mesopotamian concepts of divine wisdom (Ea/Enki as the god of wisdom whose attribute 'namtillaku' -- the wisdom of life -- was embedded in creation). However, Proverbs' Wisdom is not a goddess but an attribute of YHWH given literary personhood. The seven pillars of Wisdom's house (9:1) may reflect the seven-day creation pattern or the seven celestial bodies visible to the ancient world (Sun, Moon, Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn).

second_temple.summary: Wisdom speaks in the assembly of the Most High (divine council language) and describes her cosmic origin and journey. She finds her dwelling in Israel and is identified with 'the book of the covenant of the Most High God, the Law that Moses commanded us' (24:23).
second_temple.relevance: The most important Second Temple development of Proverbs 8. Sirach places Wisdom's speech in the divine council setting ('the assembly of the Most High') and identifies Wisdom with Torah -- a move that Proverbs itself does not make.

second_temple.summary: Wisdom is 'a breath of the power of God, and a pure emanation of the glory of the Almighty... a reflection of eternal light, a spotless mirror of the working of God' (7:25-26). She 'orders all things well' (8:1).
second_temple.relevance: Pushes Proverbs 8's personification toward a full hypostasis -- a distinct divine attribute with personal qualities. This is the bridge between Proverbs 8 and John 1's Logos christology.

second_temple.summary: 'Wisdom found no place where she might dwell; then a dwelling-place was assigned her in the heavens. Wisdom went out to make her dwelling among the children of men, but found no dwelling-place.'
second_temple.relevance: A pessimistic counterpoint to Proverbs 8's public call: in the Enochic tradition, Wisdom is rejected by humanity and returns to heaven. This tradition influenced the Johannine prologue: 'He came to his own, and his own people did not receive him' (John 1:11).

cross_refs.note: 'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God... all things were made through him' -- the Logos as the fulfillment of Proverbs 8's Wisdom
'The firstborn of all creation. For by him all things were created' -- Christ as the Wisdom present at creation
'Christ the power of God and the wisdom of God... Christ Jesus, who became to us wisdom from God' -- direct identification
The creation account that Proverbs 8:22-31 presupposes -- Wisdom was there when it happened
'Through whom also he created the world... he upholds the universe by the word of his power' -- the Son as Wisdom-Creator

prov_royal.divine_council_note: Proverbs 8:22-31 is one of the most significant divine council texts in the Bible, though it is not often recognized as such. Wisdom claims to have been 'set up' (nissakhti, 8:23) -- a term that can mean 'installed' or 'enthroned,' the same verb used for the Davidic king's installation in Psalm 2:6 ('I have set my King on Zion'). Wisdom was present 'when he established the heavens' (8:27), 'when he marked out the foundations of the earth' (8:29), and was 'beside him' (8:30) as his amon -- whether craftsman, companion, or child. In 9:10, the phrase 'knowledge of the Holy One' uses the plural 'qedoshim' (Holy Ones), which could be a plural of majesty for God or a reference to the 'holy ones' of the divine council (cf. Psalm 89:5, 'the assembly of the holy ones'). The New Testament's identification of this Wisdom with Christ (John 1; Colossians 1; Hebrews 1) means that the figure who was beside YHWH at creation, who called to humanity from the gates, and who offered life to all who would listen, is the pre-incarnate Son -- the second power in heaven.

sections.heading: Wisdom's Public Authority (8:1-21)
sections.body: Wisdom again calls from the public spaces: 'Does not wisdom call? Does not understanding raise her voice? On the heights beside the way, at the crossroads she takes her stand; beside the gates in front of the town, at the entrance of the portals she cries aloud' (8:1-3). Her speech claims supreme value: 'Take my instruction instead of silver, and knowledge rather than choice gold, for wisdom is better than jewels' (8:10-11). She claims authority over governance itself: 'By me kings reign, and rulers decree what is just; by me princes rule, and nobles, all who govern justly' (8:15-16). This is a cosmic claim: all just governance -- human and divine -- operates through wisdom. She offers love to those who love her and riches to those who seek her (8:17-21).

sections.heading: Wisdom at Creation (8:22-31)
sections.body: The theological summit: 'YHWH possessed me (qanani) at the beginning (reshit) of his work, the first of his acts of old' (8:22). The verb 'qanah' is ambiguous: 'possessed,' 'created,' or 'acquired.' The Arian controversy turned on whether this means Wisdom/Christ was created. The context favors 'possessed' or 'begot': Wisdom is not made from nothing but belongs to YHWH's own nature. 'Ages ago I was set up (nissakhti), at the first, before the beginning of the earth' (8:23). Wisdom then describes her presence at each act of creation: the heavens (8:27), the clouds (8:28), the sea's boundary (8:29), the earth's foundations (8:29). Then: 'I was beside him, like a master workman (amon); and I was daily his delight, rejoicing before him always, rejoicing in his inhabited world and delighting in the children of man' (8:30-31). This is Wisdom as God's partner in creation -- not equal to him but intimately present, delighting in the ordered world and in humanity.

sections.heading: The Two Banquets: Wisdom and Folly (9:1-18)
sections.body: Chapter 9 concludes the prologue with two parallel invitations. Woman Wisdom has 'built her house; she has hewn her seven pillars. She has slaughtered her beasts; she has mixed her wine; she has also set her table' (9:1-2). Her invitation: 'Come, eat of my bread and drink of the wine I have mixed. Leave your simple ways, and live, and walk in the way of insight' (9:5-6). Between the two invitations stands the axiom: 'The fear of YHWH is the beginning of wisdom, and the knowledge of the Holy One is insight' (9:10). Then Woman Folly mirrors the scene: she is 'loud... seductive and knows nothing' (9:13), sitting at her door calling to passersby: 'Stolen water is sweet, and bread eaten in secret is pleasant' (9:17). Her guests do not know 'that the dead are there, that her guests are in the depths of Sheol' (9:18). The choice between the two banquets is the choice between life and death, order and chaos.

prov_solomonic.title: The Righteous and the Wicked -- Antithetical Wisdom
prov_solomonic.synopsis: Chapters 10-12 are dominated by antithetical parallelism: the first line states a truth about the righteous or wise, the second line states the contrasting truth about the wicked or foolish. 'The memory of the righteous is a blessing, but the name of the wicked will rot' (10:7). These chapters establish the moral framework of the entire collection. Key themes emerge: the power of speech ('The mouth of the righteous is a fountain of life, but the mouth of the wicked conceals violence,' 10:11), the value of diligence ('A slack hand causes poverty, but the hand of the diligent makes rich,' 10:4), and YHWH's sovereign oversight ('The eyes of YHWH are in every place, keeping watch on the evil and the good'). The proverbs do not argue for the retribution principle -- they observe it as the general pattern of creation's moral order.

key_verse.text: The fear of the LORD prolongs life, but the years of the wicked will be short.

hebrew_terms: tsaddiq (righteous -- the person aligned with God's moral order)
rasha (wicked -- the person who violates God's moral order)
peh (mouth -- the instrument of speech, either fountain of life or well of violence)
charutz (diligent -- the industrious person whom wisdom commends)
prov_solomonic.ane_backdrop: The two-line antithetical proverb form is attested in Egyptian and Mesopotamian wisdom literature. The Egyptian 'Instruction of Ptahhotep' and 'Instruction of Amenemope' use similar contrastive structures. The Mesopotamian 'Counsels of Wisdom' also contrast wise and foolish behavior. However, Proverbs' consistent theological grounding -- 'the fear of YHWH' as the organizing principle -- distinguishes it from the more pragmatic orientation of most ANE wisdom texts.

second_temple.summary: Ben Sira develops similar antithetical wisdom: warnings about wealth, speech, and the contrast between faithful and unfaithful friends.
second_temple.relevance: Shows the continuation of the Proverbs wisdom tradition into the Second Temple period with similar form and content.

cross_refs.note: 'By your words you will be justified, and by your words you will be condemned' -- Jesus affirms the speech theology of Proverbs
The tongue as fire and fountain -- James develops Proverbs' extensive speech ethics
The righteous/wicked contrast -- the psalm that introduces the Psalter uses the same framework
'Whatever one sows, that will he also reap' -- the retribution principle stated in agricultural terms

prov_solomonic.divine_council_note: Proverbs 10-12 describe YHWH's active governance of the moral order: 'The eyes of YHWH are in every place, keeping watch on the evil and the good' (15:3 -- anticipated here). 'YHWH does not let the righteous go hungry, but he thwarts the craving of the wicked' (10:3). This is the retribution principle as divine council administration: YHWH actively manages the moral outcomes of human choices. The proverbs present this as the normal operation of creation's moral fabric, woven by the Wisdom who was present at creation (8:22-31).

sections.heading: The Wise Son and the Foolish Son (10:1-16)
sections.body: The collection opens: 'A wise son makes a glad father, but a foolish son is a sorrow to his mother' (10:1). This domestic starting point roots cosmic wisdom in family relationships. The proverbs then range across speech, wealth, and moral character, always in antithetical form: 'Treasures gained by wickedness do not profit, but righteousness delivers from death' (10:2); 'Whoever walks in integrity walks securely, but he who makes his ways crooked will be found out' (10:9). The density is remarkable -- each verse is a self-contained wisdom unit that rewards extended meditation.

sections.heading: Speech, Wealth, and Community (10:17-11:31)
sections.body: The power of speech dominates: 'When words are many, transgression is not lacking, but whoever restrains his lips is prudent' (10:19). Wealth is complex: 'A rich man's wealth is his strong city; the poverty of the poor is their ruin' (10:15) -- an observation, not necessarily an endorsement. The community dimension emerges: 'Where there is no guidance, a people falls, but in an abundance of counselors there is safety' (11:14). The retribution principle is stated with confidence: 'The righteous is delivered from trouble, and the wicked walks into it instead' (11:8). Yet 11:24 adds nuance: 'One gives freely, yet grows all the richer; another withholds what he should give, and only suffers want' -- generosity, paradoxically, enriches.

sections.heading: The Character of the Righteous (12:1-28)
sections.body: Chapter 12 develops the character portrait of the wise person. 'Whoever loves discipline loves knowledge, but he who hates reproof is stupid' (12:1). The righteous person is characterized by truthfulness ('Lying lips are an abomination to YHWH, but those who act faithfully are his delight,' 12:22), diligence ('The hand of the diligent will rule, while the slothful will be put to forced labor,' 12:24), and emotional restraint ('A prudent man conceals knowledge, but the heart of fools proclaims folly,' 12:23). The chapter concludes: 'In the path of righteousness is life, and in its pathway there is no death' (12:28).

prov_solomonic.title: YHWH's Oversight, Wealth, and the Tongue
prov_solomonic.synopsis: Chapters 13-15 continue the antithetical proverb collection with increasing theological depth. 'Hope deferred makes the heart sick, but a desire fulfilled is a tree of life' (13:12). The 'tree of life' allusion connects practical wisdom to Eden's eschatological promise. Chapter 14 introduces a key epistemological insight: 'There is a way that seems right to a man, but its end is the way to death' (14:12). Human moral intuition is unreliable without the fear of YHWH. Chapter 15 features some of the most frequently quoted proverbs: 'A soft answer turns away wrath, but a harsh word stirs up anger' (15:1); 'The eyes of YHWH are in every place, keeping watch on the evil and the good' (15:3); 'Better is a little with the fear of YHWH than great treasure and trouble with it' (15:16).

key_verse.text: The eyes of the LORD are in every place, keeping watch on the evil and the good.

hebrew_terms: ets chayyim (tree of life -- the Eden motif reappearing in wisdom, applied to desire fulfilled, 13:12)
toeivat YHWH (abomination to YHWH -- actions that YHWH finds detestable, a strong ethical category)
ratson YHWH (delight/favor of YHWH -- what pleases God, the positive counterpart to abomination)
marpe (healing -- the healing power of gentle speech, 15:4)
prov_solomonic.ane_backdrop: The concept of divine surveillance ('the eyes of YHWH in every place,' 15:3) is paralleled in Egyptian theology where the sun-god Re sees all things, and in Mesopotamian texts where Shamash (the sun-god of justice) sees every deed. Proverbs monotheizes this concept: it is YHWH alone who watches all.

second_temple.summary: Ben Sira insists that God does not command sin: 'Do not say, "It was the Lord's doing that I fell away." He has not done what he detests.'
second_temple.relevance: Engages with the theological tension between YHWH's sovereignty (Proverbs 15:3) and human moral responsibility.

cross_refs.note: 'No creature is hidden from his sight, but all are naked and exposed to the eyes of him to whom we must give account' -- the NT develops Proverbs 15:3
The tree of life in Eden -- Proverbs 13:12 and 15:4 use the same motif for wisdom's blessings
'If possible, so far as it depends on you, live peaceably with all' -- the soft-answer wisdom of 15:1
'There is a way that seems right to a man' -- repeated verbatim in 16:25, emphasizing this critical warning

prov_solomonic.divine_council_note: Proverbs 15:3 -- 'The eyes of YHWH are in every place, keeping watch on the evil and the good' -- presents YHWH as the omniscient sovereign who monitors all human conduct. In the divine council framework, this surveillance is exercised through YHWH's own omniscience and through the agency of his council members. Proverbs 15:11 adds: 'Sheol and Abaddon lie open before YHWH; how much more the hearts of the children of man!' Even the underworld cannot hide from YHWH's gaze. This undergirds the moral confidence of the entire proverb collection: justice is administered by an all-seeing God.

sections.heading: Wealth, Speech, and the Tree of Life (13:1-25)
sections.body: Chapter 13 continues the antithetical pattern with gems of observation: 'Wealth gained hastily will dwindle, but whoever gathers little by little will increase it' (13:11). The tree of life motif appears: 'Hope deferred makes the heart sick, but a desire fulfilled is a tree of life' (13:12). The 'tree of life' (ets chayyim) connects practical wisdom to Eden's cosmic promise -- wisdom offers in partial form what humanity lost through the fall. Speech remains central: 'Whoever guards his mouth preserves his life; he who opens wide his lips comes to ruin' (13:3).

sections.heading: The Way That Seems Right (14:1-35)
sections.body: Chapter 14 contains one of the most sobering warnings in the book: 'There is a way that seems right to a man, but its end is the way to death' (14:12). Human moral intuition is not trustworthy without the corrective of wisdom and the fear of YHWH. The chapter also contains social-justice proverbs: 'Whoever oppresses a poor man insults his Maker, but he who is generous to the needy honors him' (14:31). The Creator-creature relationship grounds social ethics: the poor bear God's image, and their mistreatment is an insult to their Maker.

sections.heading: The Eyes of YHWH and the Gentle Tongue (15:1-33)
sections.body: Chapter 15 is among the richest in the collection. The opening proverb -- 'A soft answer turns away wrath, but a harsh word stirs up anger' (15:1) -- is matched by the theological assertion: 'The eyes of YHWH are in every place, keeping watch on the evil and the good' (15:3). The 'better than' proverbs appear: 'Better is a little with the fear of YHWH than great treasure and trouble with it' (15:16); 'Better is a dinner of herbs where love is than a fattened ox and hatred with it' (15:17). These qualify the retribution principle: material prosperity is not the ultimate good. The chapter concludes with another wisdom axiom: 'The fear of YHWH is instruction in wisdom, and humility comes before honor' (15:33).

prov_solomonic.title: YHWH's Sovereignty and the King's Justice
prov_solomonic.synopsis: Chapters 16-19 are the theological core of the Solomonic collection. Chapter 16 opens with a cluster of YHWH-proverbs that assert divine sovereignty over all human plans: 'The plans of the heart belong to man, but the answer of the tongue is from YHWH' (16:1); 'The lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from YHWH' (16:33). The king appears as YHWH's representative: 'Inspired decisions are on the lips of a king; his mouth does not sin in judgment' (16:10). Pride -- the fundamental vice -- is addressed: 'Pride goes before destruction, and a haughty spirit before a fall' (16:18). Friendship, speech, and the complexity of justice continue as themes: 'A friend loves at all times, and a brother is born for adversity' (17:17); 'Even a fool who keeps silent is considered wise' (17:28).

key_verse.text: The lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from the LORD.

hebrew_terms: lev (heart -- the seat of human planning, which YHWH ultimately overrules, 16:1, 9)
gaavah (pride -- the root sin that Proverbs identifies as the path to destruction, 16:18)
goral (lot -- the casting of lots, whose every outcome YHWH determines, 16:33)
qesem (divination/inspired decision -- the king's role as channel of divine justice, 16:10)
prov_solomonic.ane_backdrop: The concept of the king as the channel of divine justice is central to ANE royal ideology. The Mesopotamian king was the 'shepherd' appointed by the gods to maintain cosmic order (misharu). The Egyptian pharaoh embodied Ma'at (cosmic order). Proverbs' royal proverbs reflect this tradition: the king's lips carry 'inspired decisions' (qesem, 16:10), suggesting oracular authority. The casting of lots (16:33) was used in both Israelite and ANE decision-making as a means of determining the divine will (cf. the Urim and Thummim, Leviticus 8:8).

second_temple.summary: Ben Sira develops the theme of divine sovereignty: 'Good is the opposite of evil, and life the opposite of death... Look upon all the works of the Most High; they come in pairs, one the opposite of the other.'
second_temple.relevance: Develops Proverbs' antithetical wisdom into a broader theological framework of divine ordering of opposites.

cross_refs.note: 'You do not know what tomorrow will bring' -- James echoes 16:1, 9 on human planning and divine sovereignty
The apostles cast lots to choose Matthias -- the practice affirmed in 16:33 as under YHWH's control
'God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble' (quoting Prov 3:34) -- the anti-pride theme of 16:18
Governing authority as God's servant for justice -- developing the royal proverbs' theology

prov_solomonic.divine_council_note: The YHWH-proverbs of chapter 16 present the most concentrated statement of divine sovereignty in the wisdom literature. 'The plans of the heart belong to man, but the answer of the tongue is from YHWH' (16:1). 'YHWH has made everything for its purpose, even the wicked for the day of trouble' (16:4). 'The lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from YHWH' (16:33). This is the divine council worldview applied to everyday life: nothing escapes YHWH's governance. Even seemingly random events (the casting of lots) are under divine control. The king who governs justly participates in the divine council's administration of justice on earth.

sections.heading: YHWH's Sovereignty Over Human Plans (16:1-33)
sections.body: Chapter 16 opens with a stunning cluster of YHWH-proverbs: 'The plans of the heart belong to man, but the answer of the tongue is from YHWH' (16:1). 'Commit your work to YHWH, and your plans will be established' (16:3). 'YHWH has made everything for its purpose, even the wicked for the day of trouble' (16:4). 'The heart of man plans his way, but YHWH establishes his steps' (16:9). This is sovereignty theology in proverbial form: human initiative is real but operates within divine governance. The famous 16:18 warns: 'Pride goes before destruction, and a haughty spirit before a fall.' The chapter closes with the lot proverb (16:33), establishing that even randomness is divinely directed.

sections.heading: Friendship, Conflict, and the Fool (17:1-18:24)
sections.body: These chapters explore social relationships with penetrating insight. 'Better is a dry morsel with quiet than a house full of feasting with strife' (17:1). 'A friend loves at all times, and a brother is born for adversity' (17:17). The fool is diagnosed: 'A rebuke goes deeper into a man of understanding than a hundred blows into a fool' (17:10); 'Even a fool who keeps silent is considered wise; when he closes his lips, he is deemed intelligent' (17:28). Chapter 18 adds: 'A man who isolates himself seeks his own desire; he breaks out against all sound judgment' (18:1). The chapter culminates: 'The name of YHWH is a strong tower; the righteous man runs into it and is safe' (18:10).

sections.heading: Poverty, Justice, and Domestic Life (19:1-29)
sections.body: Chapter 19 addresses poverty with sympathy and complexity: 'Better is a poor person who walks in his integrity than one who is crooked in speech and is a fool' (19:1). Poverty attracts neither friends nor favor: 'Wealth brings many new friends, but a poor man is deserted by his friend' (19:4). Social justice is grounded theologically: 'Whoever is generous to the poor lends to YHWH, and he will repay him for his deed' (19:17). The family proverbs are vivid: 'A foolish son is ruin to his father, and a wife's quarreling is a continual dripping of rain' (19:13). Discipline of children is advocated: 'Discipline your son, for there is hope; do not set your heart on putting him to death' (19:18).

prov_solomonic.title: The Close of the Solomonic Collection
prov_solomonic.synopsis: The Solomonic collection concludes with proverbs on kingship, justice, and the heart. 'The king's heart is a stream of water in the hand of YHWH; he turns it wherever he will' (21:1) -- even royal power is under divine sovereignty. 'Every way of a man is right in his own eyes, but YHWH weighs the heart' (21:2) -- human self-assessment is unreliable. 'To do righteousness and justice is more acceptable to YHWH than sacrifice' (21:3) -- echoing the prophetic critique of empty ritual (Isaiah 1:11-17; Micah 6:6-8). The section closes with 22:1-16, including: 'A good name is to be chosen rather than great riches, and favor is better than silver or gold' (22:1); 'Train up a child in the way he should go; even when he is old he will not depart from it' (22:6).

key_verse.text: The king's heart is a stream of water in the hand of the LORD; he turns it wherever he will.

hebrew_terms: palge mayim (streams of water -- the irrigation-channel metaphor for YHWH directing the king's will)
taqan (to weigh/test -- YHWH's evaluation of the heart, beyond human self-assessment, 21:2)
shem tov (good name -- reputation and character as more valuable than wealth, 22:1)
chanakh (to train/dedicate -- the education of the child in wisdom's path, 22:6)
prov_solomonic.ane_backdrop: The irrigation metaphor of 21:1 ('the king's heart is a stream of water in YHWH's hand') draws on the agricultural reality of ancient irrigation: channels were directed by the farmer to water specific fields. The image presents even the most powerful human (the king) as a channel YHWH directs at will. The preference for justice over sacrifice (21:3) echoes a theme found across the prophetic tradition and in Egyptian wisdom (the Instruction of Merikare: 'More acceptable is the character of one upright of heart than the ox of the evildoer').

second_temple.summary: A collection of ethical instructions covering speech, friendship, family, and worship -- developing the same themes as Proverbs 20-22:16.
second_temple.relevance: Demonstrates the ongoing vitality of the proverbial wisdom tradition into the Hellenistic period.

cross_refs.note: 'YHWH stirred up the spirit of Cyrus king of Persia' -- the historical illustration of 21:1
'To obey is better than sacrifice' -- the prophetic parallel to 21:3
The widow's mite -- Jesus values the heart over the amount, as 21:2-3 implies
'Bring them up in the discipline and instruction of the Lord' -- the NT form of 22:6

prov_solomonic.divine_council_note: Proverbs 21:1 is one of the strongest sovereignty statements in the wisdom literature: 'The king's heart is a stream of water in the hand of YHWH; he turns it wherever he will.' In the divine council framework, this means that even human political power operates under YHWH's sovereign direction. The kings of the earth may rage (Psalm 2:1-2), but their hearts are channels YHWH directs. Proverbs 21:30 makes the point absolute: 'No wisdom, no understanding, no counsel can avail against YHWH.' No human plan and no spiritual strategy can override the divine council's sovereign.

sections.heading: Wine, the King, and Divine Sovereignty (20:1-30)
sections.body: Chapter 20 opens: 'Wine is a mocker, strong drink a brawler, and whoever is led astray by it is not wise' (20:1). The king's authority is emphasized: 'The terror of a king is like the growling of a lion; whoever provokes him to anger forfeits his life' (20:2). Human self-knowledge is limited: 'Who can say, "I have made my heart pure; I am clean from my sin"?' (20:9). The chapter contains practical commercial ethics: 'Unequal weights and unequal measures are both alike an abomination to YHWH' (20:10, 23). YHWH's oversight is comprehensive: 'The spirit (neshamah) of man is the lamp of YHWH, searching all his innermost parts' (20:27).

sections.heading: The King's Heart and Justice Over Sacrifice (21:1-31)
sections.body: Chapter 21 opens with the sovereignty-of-the-king proverb (21:1) and the heart-weighing proverb (21:2), establishing YHWH's absolute authority over all human power and self-assessment. The prophetic wisdom tradition emerges: 'To do righteousness and justice is more acceptable to YHWH than sacrifice' (21:3). The chapter diagnoses various forms of folly: the proud (21:4, 24), the lazy (21:25-26), the violent (21:7), and the contentious wife (21:9, 19). The conclusion is absolute: 'The horse is made ready for the day of battle, but the victory belongs to YHWH' (21:31).

sections.heading: A Good Name, the Child, and the Close (22:1-16)
sections.body: The final section of the Solomonic collection contains some of the most beloved proverbs. 'A good name is to be chosen rather than great riches, and favor is better than silver or gold' (22:1). 'The rich and the poor meet together; YHWH is the Maker of them all' (22:2) -- social-economic equality grounded in creation theology. 'Train up a child in the way he should go; even when he is old he will not depart from it' (22:6) -- the foundational education proverb (though 'the way he should go' may mean 'according to his own way/nature,' i.e., personalized education). The collection closes at 22:16 with a warning: 'Whoever oppresses the poor to increase his own wealth, or gives to the rich, will only come to poverty.'

prov_collections.title: The Words of the Wise -- The Amenemope Connection
prov_collections.synopsis: This section opens: 'Incline your ear, and hear the words of the wise, and apply your heart to my knowledge' (22:17). The 'thirty sayings' (22:20, emending 'excellent things' to 'thirty' -- shillshom to sheloshim) closely parallel the Egyptian 'Instruction of Amenemope' (~1200 BC), which also contains thirty chapters of wisdom. The parallels include: do not exploit the poor (22:22 // Amenemope ch. 2), do not befriend a hot-tempered man (22:24 // Amenemope ch. 9), do not move ancient boundary stones (22:28 // Amenemope ch. 6), and the image of riches sprouting wings and flying away (23:5 // Amenemope ch. 7). This is not plagiarism but wisdom exchange: Israel's sages participated in an international wisdom dialogue, shared truth across cultures, and received it under the authority of YHWH. Chapter 24 concludes with additional sayings on justice, diligence, and the avoidance of gloating over enemies.

key_verse.text: Do not rob the poor, because he is poor, or crush the afflicted at the gate, for the LORD will plead their cause and rob of life those who rob them.

hebrew_terms: sheloshim (thirty -- the thirty sayings that parallel Amenemope's thirty chapters)
gevul olam (ancient boundary stone -- the property marker that must not be moved, protecting inheritance)
riv (to plead a case -- YHWH as the legal advocate of the poor, 22:23)
dal (poor/weak -- the vulnerable person whom YHWH defends against exploitation)
prov_collections.ane_backdrop: The parallel between Proverbs 22:17-23:11 and the Egyptian 'Instruction of Amenemope' is the most extensively documented case of literary dependence between a biblical text and an ANE source. Amenemope was composed ~1200 BC by a scribe named Amenemope for his son. The text contains thirty chapters of wisdom advice. At least ten specific parallels have been identified with the Proverbs passage, often in the same order. Three views exist: (1) Proverbs borrows from Amenemope; (2) both draw from a common ANE wisdom tradition; (3) Amenemope borrows from an earlier Israelite source. View (1) is most widely held among scholars, but the theological significance is the same regardless: Israel's wisdom tradition was not isolated but engaged with the best thinking of the ancient world, under the conviction that all truth ultimately derives from the God who 'by wisdom founded the earth' (3:19).

second_temple.summary: Ben Sira develops the theme of defending the poor: 'Do not reject a supplicant in distress... do not turn your face away from the poor.'
second_temple.relevance: Shows the continuation of the Proverbs social-justice tradition into the Second Temple period.

cross_refs.note: Paul quotes Proverbs 25:21-22 (from the next collection): 'If your enemy is hungry, feed him' -- applying wisdom to enemy love
Torah protections for the poor, widow, and orphan -- the legal foundation for the wisdom tradition's social justice
Partiality toward the rich dishonors the poor whom God has chosen -- developing the wisdom tradition's defense of the vulnerable
'You shall not move your neighbor's landmark' -- the Torah command behind 22:28

prov_collections.divine_council_note: Proverbs 22:22-23 presents YHWH as the goel (kinsman-redeemer) of the poor: 'YHWH will plead their cause (riv) and rob of life those who rob them.' The legal terminology (riv = lawsuit) places YHWH in the divine court as the advocate for the defenseless. In the divine council framework, Psalm 82:2-4 indicts the gods of the nations for failing to 'give justice to the weak and the fatherless; maintain the right of the afflicted and the destitute.' What the corrupt council members failed to do, YHWH does directly: he becomes the defender of the poor against their oppressors.

sections.heading: The Thirty Sayings and the Amenemope Connection (22:17-23:11)
sections.body: The section opens with an instruction to 'hear the words of the wise' and contains the 'thirty sayings' (22:20). The parallels with Amenemope are striking: do not rob the poor 'at the gate' (the place of legal proceedings, 22:22), do not associate with the hot-tempered (22:24), do not remove ancient landmarks (22:28). The warning about riches is vivid: 'Do not toil to acquire wealth; be discerning enough to desist. When your eyes light on it, it is gone, for suddenly it sprouts wings, flying like an eagle toward heaven' (23:4-5). Amenemope uses the same image: wealth 'has made itself wings like geese and has flown to heaven.' The theological appropriation is clear: truth observed by Egyptian sages is valid because YHWH's wisdom is universal.

sections.heading: Family, Self-Control, and Further Wisdom (23:12-24:22)
sections.body: The instruction turns to family relationships: 'Listen to your father who gave you life, and do not despise your mother when she is old' (23:22). Warnings against drunkenness are vivid: 'Who has woe? Who has sorrow?... Those who tarry long over wine' (23:29-30). The drunkard's experience is described with dark humor: 'Your eyes will see strange things, and your heart utter perverse things. You will be like one who lies down in the midst of the sea' (23:33-34). Chapter 24 counsels against envying the wicked (24:1, 19-20), advocates for honest witness (24:28), and the section closes with the famous vineyard parable: the lazy man's field is overgrown with thorns (24:30-34).

sections.heading: Additional Sayings of the Wise (24:23-34)
sections.body: A brief appendix of wise sayings beginning with 'These also are sayings of the wise' (24:23). The emphasis is on impartial justice: 'He who says to the wicked, "You are in the right," will be cursed by peoples, abhorred by nations, but those who rebuke the wicked will have delight' (24:24-25). The concluding vineyard parable (24:30-34) teaches by negative example: the lazy man's field produces only thorns and poverty. 'A little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the hands to rest, and poverty will come upon you like a robber' (24:33-34).

prov_collections.title: The Hezekiah Collection -- Solomonic Proverbs Recovered
prov_collections.synopsis: This collection has a unique historical superscription: 'These also are proverbs of Solomon which the men of Hezekiah king of Judah copied out (he'etiqu)' (25:1). Hezekiah's scribes, working ~700 BC -- about 250 years after Solomon -- gathered, edited, and transmitted Solomonic wisdom. This is the only internal evidence of a specific editorial process in the biblical wisdom literature. The proverbs here tend to be longer and more complex than those in 10-22:16, with more figurative language and situational applications. Key themes include royal conduct (25:2-7), speech ethics (25:11-15; 26:17-28), the fool and the sluggard (26:1-16), friendship and conflict (27:1-22), and the contrast between righteous and wicked governance (28:1-29:27).

key_verse.text: If your enemy is hungry, give him bread to eat, and if he is thirsty, give him water to drink, for you will heap burning coals on his head, and the LORD will reward you.

hebrew_terms: he'etiqu (copied out/transcribed -- the editorial activity of Hezekiah's scribes, 25:1)
gachalim (burning coals -- the image of kindness to enemies that 'heaps coals' on them, 25:22)
kesil (fool -- the character type extensively analyzed in chapter 26)
atsel (sluggard -- the lazy person whose portrait is drawn with biting humor, 26:13-16)
prov_collections.ane_backdrop: The practice of royal scribal schools copying and transmitting wisdom texts is well attested in the ANE. Mesopotamian scribal schools preserved and transmitted the 'Counsels of Wisdom,' the 'Instructions of Shuruppak,' and other texts over centuries. Egyptian scribes similarly copied and updated the 'Instructions' of Ptahhotep, Amenemhet, and others. Hezekiah's scribes (25:1) were participating in this established ANE literary practice: preserving the wisdom of a great king for future generations. Hezekiah's era was a period of literary activity (cf. the Siloam Inscription) and religious reform (2 Kings 18-20).

second_temple.summary: Ben Sira describes the scribe's vocation: 'The wisdom of the scribe depends on the opportunity of leisure; only the one who has little business can become wise.'
second_temple.relevance: Provides the Second Temple context for understanding scribal wisdom transmission like that described in 25:1.

cross_refs.note: Paul directly quotes Proverbs 25:21-22: 'If your enemy is hungry, feed him' -- the wisdom of enemy love
'Love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you' -- Jesus fulfills the Proverbs ethic Paul quotes
Hezekiah's reform and literary activity -- the historical context for the scribal work of 25:1
Quotes Proverbs 26:11: 'A dog returns to its vomit' -- applied to apostates

prov_collections.divine_council_note: Proverbs 25:2 contains a striking statement about the relationship between divine and human knowledge: 'It is the glory of God to conceal things, but the glory of kings is to search things out.' God's sovereignty includes hiddenness -- there are dimensions of his governance that are deliberately concealed from human view (cf. Deuteronomy 29:29, 'The secret things belong to YHWH our God'). The king's role (and by extension, the wise person's role) is to search out what can be known. In the divine council framework, this means that wisdom involves penetrating as far as possible into the hidden workings of YHWH's governance -- while accepting that some things remain forever beyond human comprehension (cf. Job 38-41).

sections.heading: Royal Wisdom and Speech Ethics (25:1-28)
sections.body: The editorial superscription (25:1) provides a rare window into the process of biblical compilation. The proverbs begin with royal instruction: 'It is the glory of God to conceal things, but the glory of kings is to search things out' (25:2). Speech is central: 'A word fitly spoken is like apples of gold in a setting of silver' (25:11). The enemy-love proverb (25:21-22) is striking: feeding your hungry enemy and giving him water will 'heap burning coals on his head' -- probably an image of the burning shame that transforms hostility into reconciliation. Paul's quotation of this proverb in Romans 12:20 places it at the heart of Christian ethics.

sections.heading: The Fool, the Sluggard, and the Gossip (26:1-28)
sections.body: Chapter 26 provides the most sustained analysis of the fool (kesil) and the sluggard (atsel) in the Bible. The paradoxical pair of 26:4-5 is famous: 'Answer not a fool according to his folly, lest you be like him yourself. Answer a fool according to his folly, lest he be wise in his own eyes.' Both are true: situational wisdom requires knowing when to engage and when to walk away. The sluggard portrait is darkly humorous: 'The sluggard says, "There is a lion in the road! There is a lion in the streets!" As a door turns on its hinges, so does a sluggard on his bed' (26:13-14). The chapter concludes with warnings about gossip and flattery (26:20-28).

sections.heading: Friendship, Self-Knowledge, and Just Governance (27:1-29:27)
sections.body: Chapter 27 offers rich relational wisdom: 'Faithful are the wounds of a friend; profuse are the kisses of an enemy' (27:6). 'Iron sharpens iron, and one man sharpens another' (27:17). 'As in water face reflects face, so the heart of man reflects the man' (27:19). Chapters 28-29 contrast righteous and wicked governance: 'When the righteous triumph, there is great glory, but when the wicked rise, people hide themselves' (28:12). 'The righteous care about justice for the poor, but the wicked have no such concern' (29:7). The collection closes: 'Where there is no prophetic vision the people cast off restraint, but blessed is he who keeps the law (torah)' (29:18) -- connecting wisdom to prophetic revelation and Torah obedience.

prov_collections.title: Agur, Lemuel, and the Valiant Woman
prov_collections.synopsis: The final two chapters present three distinct voices. Agur son of Jakeh (30:1-33) opens with radical humility: 'Surely I am too stupid to be a man. I have not the understanding of a man. I have not learned wisdom, nor have I knowledge of the Holy One' (30:2-3). He then asks questions echoing YHWH's challenge to Job: 'Who has ascended to heaven and come down?... What is his name, and what is his son's name?' (30:4). His 'numerical proverbs' are masterpieces of observational wisdom (30:15-31). King Lemuel's oracle (31:1-9), taught by his mother, warns against women, wine, and injustice. The book concludes with the acrostic poem on the eshet chayil -- the 'woman of valor' (31:10-31) -- who embodies everything the book has taught: industry, generosity, wisdom, strength, and the fear of YHWH.

key_verse.text: Charm is deceitful, and beauty is vain, but a woman who fears the LORD is to be praised.

hebrew_terms: massa (oracle/burden -- the genre designation for both Agur's and Lemuel's speeches)
eshet chayil (woman of valor/strength -- the military term applied to the ideal woman, 31:10)
qedoshim (Holy One/Ones -- Agur's reference to divine knowledge, 30:3, matching 9:10)
aleph-tav acrostic (the Proverbs 31 poem uses all 22 Hebrew letters in order)
prov_collections.ane_backdrop: The numerical proverb form ('three things... four') is attested in Ugaritic literature (the Baal Cycle uses x/x+1 patterns) and in Amos 1-2 ('for three transgressions... and for four'). The queen-mother instruction genre (Lemuel's oracle, 31:1-9) has parallels in Egyptian royal instruction texts where the mother or father instructs the future king. The acrostic form is a sophisticated literary device found in several ANE literary traditions and in multiple biblical texts (Psalms 111, 112, 119; Lamentations 1-4; Nahum 1:2-8).

second_temple.summary: Ben Sira's praise of the good wife: 'Happy is the husband of a good wife; the number of his days will be doubled. A loyal wife brings joy to her husband.'
second_temple.relevance: Develops the Proverbs 31 tradition of praising the virtuous wife, though with a more patriarchal lens than the Proverbs original.

second_temple.summary: A fragmentary wisdom text beginning with beatitude formulas ('Happy is the man who...') that celebrates the pursuit of wisdom in terms echoing Proverbs' personification.
second_temple.relevance: Shows the Qumran community's engagement with the personified-wisdom tradition, bridging between Proverbs and the Beatitudes of Matthew 5.

cross_refs.note: 'No one has ascended into heaven except he who descended from heaven, the Son of Man' -- Jesus answers Agur's question of 30:4
Boaz calls Ruth an 'eshet chayil' (woman of valor) -- the same term used in Proverbs 31:10
'Let your adorning be the hidden person of the heart' -- echoing 31:30's preference for character over beauty
The Bride of the Lamb -- the eschatological fulfillment of the Proverbs 31 woman as the people of God adorned in righteousness
The wise and foolish virgins -- Jesus' parable using the same woman-wisdom/folly framework

prov_collections.divine_council_note: Agur's questions (30:4) have profound divine council implications: 'Who has ascended to heaven and come down? Who has gathered the wind in his fists? Who has wrapped up the waters in a garment? Who has established all the ends of the earth? What is his name, and what is his son's name? Surely you know!' The questions about 'his name' and 'his son's name' are extraordinary. They presuppose that the Creator has a 'son' -- a figure who shares in or has access to the divine identity. In the divine council framework, this anticipates the 'two powers in heaven' theology: YHWH and his unique Son who shares his throne (Psalm 2:7; 110:1). Jesus answers Agur's question in John 3:13: 'No one has ascended into heaven except he who descended from heaven, the Son of Man.'

sections.heading: Agur's Humility and the Son's Name (30:1-14)
sections.body: Agur opens with disarming humility: 'I am too stupid to be a man' (30:2). His questions echo YHWH's challenge to Job (38:4-7): 'Who has ascended to heaven and come down?... What is his name, and what is his son's name?' (30:4). The reference to 'his son's name' is unique in the Old Testament wisdom tradition and anticipates the christological development of the 'Son of God' concept. Agur then affirms the reliability of God's word: 'Every word of God proves true; he is a shield to those who take refuge in him. Do not add to his words, lest he rebuke you and you be found a liar' (30:5-6). His prayer is beautifully modest: 'Give me neither poverty nor riches; feed me with the food that is needful for me, lest I be full and deny you and say, "Who is YHWH?" or lest I be poor and steal and profane the name of my God' (30:8-9).

sections.heading: Numerical Proverbs: The Wonder of Creation (30:15-33)
sections.body: Agur's numerical proverbs are masterpieces of observational wisdom. 'Three things are too wonderful for me; four I do not understand: the way of an eagle in the sky, the way of a serpent on a rock, the way of a ship on the high seas, and the way of a man with a virgin' (30:18-19). All four describe graceful, mysterious movement that leaves no trace -- the wonder of effortless mastery. Other numerical sayings address the earth-shaking (30:21-23), the small but wise (ants, rock badgers, locusts, lizards -- 30:24-28), and the stately in stride (lion, rooster, he-goat, king -- 30:29-31). These observations celebrate the wisdom embedded in creation itself -- the same wisdom that was beside YHWH at creation (8:30-31).

sections.heading: Lemuel's Oracle and the Eshet Chayil (31:1-31)
sections.body: King Lemuel's oracle, taught by his mother (31:1-9), warns against dissipation (wine, women) and charges the king to 'open your mouth for the mute, for the rights of all who are destitute. Open your mouth, judge righteously, defend the rights of the poor and needy' (31:8-9). Then the acrostic poem celebrates the eshet chayil ('woman of valor,' 31:10-31). She is far more than domestic: she trades in real estate (31:16), runs a textile business (31:24), 'opens her mouth with wisdom, and the teaching of kindness is on her tongue' (31:26), and her household is ordered by her diligence. Many scholars read the eshet chayil as the incarnation of Lady Wisdom from chapters 1-9: wisdom embodied in a human life. The concluding verse is the book's final word: 'Charm is deceitful, and beauty is vain, but a woman who fears YHWH is to be praised' (31:30). The book ends where it began: the fear of YHWH.
